response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental incompatibility between your .NET 7 cross-platform project and the WPF libraries, which are strictly designed for the Windows-specific .NET Framework or .NET Core/.NET 5+ Windows targets. When your application attempts to load the WPF assembly on a system where it is not properly referenced or configured for the current runtime, the CLR fails to locate the specific version of PresentationFramework, resulting in the file not found exception.

To resolve this issue without abandoning your cross-platform architecture, you must ensure that your WPF project is explicitly targeted for Windows and conditionally compiled only when the runtime detects a Windows environment. You can achieve this by using SDK-style project files with conditional imports or by utilizing the `Condition` attribute in your main project's `.csproj` file to include the WPF references solely when the `$(OS)` or `$(TargetFramework)` indicates a Windows build, thereby preventing the Mac build from attempting to load these Windows-only binaries.

cousin projects in the .NET ecosystem often face similar challenges when mixing platform-specific UI frameworks, so the most robust solution is to abstract your UI logic behind a shared interface that is implemented separately by your WPF and Cocoa modules. By defining a common contract in a class library that targets .NET Standard or .NET 7 without any UI dependencies, you allow the main application to remain platform-agnostic while delegating the actual window creation and rendering to the platform-specific implementations that are only compiled and loaded on their respective operating systems.

Implementing this pattern requires you to refactor your code so that the main application never directly references `System.Windows` namespaces or WPF assemblies; instead, it should interact with your abstract UI service that injects either the WPF window handler on Windows or the Cocoa window handler on macOS. This approach ensures that when the application runs on a Mac, the WPF assembly is never requested by the dependency resolver, completely eliminating the possibility of the `FileNotFoundException` occurring on non-Windows platforms.

Finally, verify your project structure by ensuring that the WPF project is set to target `net7.0-windows` while your main application and shared libraries target `net7.0` without the Windows suffix, and use build configurations or conditional compilation symbols to control which UI project gets built for each platform. This configuration allows Visual Studio or the command-line build tools to skip the WPF compilation entirely on macOS, satisfying your requirement for a cross-platform app that uses native UI technologies on each OS without forcing a downgrade
